title: SEGMENT 13 — Limitations: what synthetic embryos don't do

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Limitations: what synthetic embryos don't do
Let me spend a segment on what synthetic embryos can't do, because the limitations are real and important and they bear directly on both the scientific applications and the ethical assessment.
Limitation one: low efficiency.
I've mentioned this several times.
Across essentially every synthetic embryo protocol, only a minority of starting aggregates develops into well-formed structures.
Reported efficiencies vary wildly depending on what you count — sometimes a few percent, sometimes tens of percent for the simpler structures like blastoids.
For the most advanced post-implantation models, the proportion of starting aggregates that develop into anything resembling a proper mid-development structure is small.
The rest fail — they're disorganized, they're missing lineages, they don't form the right cavities, they don't acquire the right axes.
This means a lot of starting material goes to waste, and it means that the structures used for downstream experiments are a selected, possibly atypical subset of all the things you could have made.
This is not a flaw per se — many biological experiments work this way — but it's an important caveat.
Limitation two: anatomical drift.
Even the well-formed synthetic embryos aren't perfect copies of real embryos.

They have characteristic drifts — cell type proportions that are slightly off, particular structures that are over- or under-represented, axes that don't always align with the conventions of real development.

A mouse synthetic embryo at day 8.5, for example, might have the right structures but with slightly distorted proportions, or with particular cell types in slightly wrong positions, or with axis orientations that vary from specimen to specimen.
The structures are recognizable models, not photographic reproductions.
Limitation three: incomplete cell type coverage.
Real embryos contain not just the major lineages but many specialized cell types within each lineage.
Real trophoblast, by mid-development, has differentiated into syncytiotrophoblast and extravillous trophoblast and various other subtypes.
Real epiblast gives rise to highly specialized cells of each germ layer.
Synthetic embryos capture the major populations but often miss the specialized subtypes, or get them in wrong proportions, or get them on wrong timing.
This matters when you're trying to model a particular developmental event that depends on these subtypes.
Limitation four: developmental time ceiling.
The most striking demonstration we have, Hanna's mouse synthetic embryos, gets to day 8.5.
Real mouse development goes to day 19 to 20.
So even at the cutting edge, synthetic embryos cover less than half of mouse development.
Human synthetic embryos cover even less — currently into the early post-implantation period, but not yet into anything close to organogenesis.
Pushing the time ceiling further requires solving multiple technical problems simultaneously — better culture systems, vascular support, the right signaling at each stage.
Progress is happening, but slowly.
Limitation five: no real placenta, no maternal interface.
A real pregnancy is a complex interaction between embryo and mother — the maternal blood supply, the maternal immune system, the uterine environment, the hormonal cross-talk.
Synthetic embryos in dishes don't have any of this.
The trophoblast cells are present, and they do some of the things real trophoblast does, but they're not attached to a real uterus.
They don't get maternal blood.

Many aspects of normal development that depend on the maternal interface — proper vascular development, immune tolerance establishment, certain hormonal feedback loops — are missing or distorted in the dish.
Limitation six: variability between labs.
I've mentioned this for blastoids, but it applies to all synthetic embryo work.
Different protocols give different results.
A "synthetic embryo" from one lab is not necessarily directly comparable to a "synthetic embryo" from another lab.
This is normal for a young field — protocols haven't standardized, the field is still figuring out what matters and what doesn't — but it makes cross-comparison and meta-analysis harder.
Limitation seven: incomplete recapitulation of human-specific biology.
Most of the most advanced synthetic embryo work has been done in mouse.
Translating those successes to human cells is technically demanding, and even successful human work currently lags well behind what's possible in mouse.
We have human blastoids; we have human early-post-implantation structures; we don't yet have human equivalents of Hanna's day-8.5 mouse synthetic embryos.
The cell biology of humans differs from mouse in important ways — the timing of events, the signaling pathways involved, the relative importance of different lineages.
So scaling up from mouse to human is not just a matter of changing the cells.
These limitations matter for two reasons.
Scientifically, they bound what you can conclude from synthetic embryo experiments.
If you treat a synthetic embryo with a drug and see a particular effect, you have to be careful about extrapolating to real embryos, especially across species.
The model is a model.
It tells you something, but it tells you something less than the thing being modeled.
Ethically, they bound how seriously the structures should be taken as moral analogs of real embryos.
The position that synthetic embryos should be regulated like real embryos depends, at least in part, on how closely they resemble real embryos.
If synthetic embryos have major developmental drifts, can't make it past a particular stage, can't really continue developing into a fetus, then the case for treating them like real embryos weakens.
Conversely, as the technology improves and the limitations shrink, the case for stricter regulation strengthens.
This is the tension at the heart of the field.
The better the model is, the more useful it is — but also the more it triggers the ethical concerns that the limitations had been holding at bay.
As synthetic embryos get better, the ethics has to get more careful.
As the limitations shrink, the regulatory questions intensify.
For now, in 2025, the limitations are substantial.
Synthetic embryos are useful models, but they are clearly models.
They are not, by any reasonable measure, indistinguishable from real embryos.
That's why most regulatory frameworks treat them somewhat differently — typically with comparable oversight but not identical legal status.
As the technology improves, the frameworks will have to update too.
We're going to come back to this in Segment 14, with a slightly different framing.
